key_insight: Molecular dynamics shows that nanoparticle size controls thermal shape stability in polymeric nanofluids, with 28-atom particles destabilizing at higher temperature while 42- and 56-atom particles remain structurally stable.
engineering_relevance: This helps guide nanoparticle sizing and fluid-particle affinity design for nanofluids that must retain structure under operating temperature changes.
method: Molecular dynamics simulation of soft, nonrigid nanoparticles in a polymer melt
Computed one-dimensional RDF and three-dimensional angle-dependent RDF (ARDF)
Compared particles of 28, 42, and 56 atoms with and without the base fluid across temperatures
evidence_signal: The abstract reports structural response trends from RDF/ARDF analysis, showing shell breakdown for 28-atom nanoparticles from T=1.2 to T=1.8, but preserved concentric shells for 42- and 56-atom particles.
watch_item: The conclusions are based on simulation results for specific model sizes and interaction conditions, so transferability to real nanofluids depends on how well the force field and polymer-particle affinity represent the target system.
paper_title: The Radial Distribution Functions of Nanofluids: Molecular Dynamics Simulations
